summary: I graduated from Niles West High School in 1965. It was my local school in Niles, and I had friends there who also enlisted in the military. I remember that four of my high school classmates never made it back from the war. Attending Niles West was the start of my journey before I joined the Marine Corps.

My dad served in the United States Army Air Forces during World War II. He was stationed in Okinawa, and although he didn't really approve of me joining the military, his service was part of my family's history. His experience influenced me somewhat, even if I chose a different branch. It was a legacy that I was aware of when I enlisted.

I enlisted in the United States Marine Corps in August 1966 when I was just 17 years old. I chose the Marines because I was inspired by stories of their World War II exploits and the Marine Corps Hymn. I signed up with a buddy, but he didn't show up, so I went alone. My training was tough, especially being so young and away from home, but I was proud to serve in the Marines during the Vietnam War.

When I was sent from Chicago to San Diego for boot camp, I flew on a Continental Airlines plane. That was my first time away from home for any length of time, and it felt like a great adventure. The flight was part of the beginning of my Marine Corps journey in 1966.

After boot camp in San Diego, I was sent to Camp Pendleton for artillery training. We learned to operate mortars, 105mm, and 155mm artillery pieces, and did infantry training including crawling through barbed wire and dealing with simulated explosions. It was a tough but essential part of my preparation before going overseas. I remember the training was realistic and demanding.

After Camp Pendleton, I was transferred to Camp Lejeune where I stayed for about three or four months. I got more training there with the 2nd or 3rd Marine Division, and it was a bit easier compared to Pendleton. My dad and Arlen got married in November 1966, and I made it home for the wedding before heading back to Camp Lejeune. From there, I was sent to Vietnam in May 1967.

The 18th Infantry Regiment is mentioned in my records, but my main focus was artillery. I don't recall serving directly with the 18th Infantry Regiment, but they were part of the broader Marine operations during my time. My role was primarily with artillery units, so my contact with infantry regiments was limited.

entity: United States Marine Corps 3rd Marine Division
summary: I was assigned to the 3rd Marine Division when I arrived in Vietnam. Specifically, I was part of Alpha Battery, 1st Battalion, 3rd Marine Division from June 1967 until I left Vietnam. The division was involved in major operations like the Defense of Hue and the Battle of Khe Sanh. Serving with the 3rd Marine Division was intense but a defining part of my military career.

When I first arrived in Vietnam, I was assigned to Charlie Battery and stationed at Phu Bai for about a month. Charlie Battery was my initial unit before I was transferred to Alpha Battery at Khe Sanh. My time with Charlie Battery included participating in the Defense of Hue and getting acclimated to combat conditions in Vietnam.

After arriving at Khe Sanh in June 1967, I was transferred to Alpha Battery, which became my main unit. I served in Alpha Battery, 1st Platoon, 3rd Marine Division from June 1967 until I left Vietnam. Alpha Battery operated 105mm and 155mm howitzers, and we were heavily involved in the Battle of Khe Sanh and other operations. It was a challenging but important part of my service.

I served in the Vietnam War from May 1967 until I left in 1968. My deployment included time at Phu Bai, the Defense of Hue, and the Battle of Khe Sanh. The war was intense and difficult, especially being so young and in artillery. It was a defining experience in my life and military career.

I participated in the Defense of Hue while stationed at Phu Bai with Charlie Battery. This was one of the major engagements during my time in Vietnam in early 1967. The fighting was intense, and it was a critical moment in the war. Being part of the artillery support during the defense was a significant part of my combat experience.

Phu Bai was my first station in Vietnam where I served with Charlie Battery for about a month. We operated artillery there and participated in the Defense of Hue. The conditions were tough but it was a key location before moving on to Khe Sanh. Traveling to Phu Bai was by truck from Da Nang.

I was at Khe Sanh starting June 14, 1967, where I served with Alpha Battery, 3rd Marine Division. The Battle of Khe Sanh was one of the most intense parts of my service, involving heavy artillery operations and constant threat from enemy forces. Life at Khe Sanh was harsh, but we held our ground. It was a defining battle of the Vietnam War and my military career.

Operation Crockett took place around June 14, 1967, and I was involved in it while at Khe Sanh. This operation was part of the larger effort to defend the base and surrounding areas. I was transferred to Alpha Battery during this time and participated in artillery support for the operation. It was a critical moment in the buildup to the Battle of Khe Sanh.

Pardee's Pirates was not directly mentioned in my service, so I don't have personal experience with that group. My focus was on artillery units within the Marine Corps during Vietnam. If Pardee's Pirates were active, I didn't serve with them or have direct contact.

Robert Stack was not someone I encountered or mentioned during my military service. My experiences were focused on Marine Corps training and combat in Vietnam, so I don't have any stories or connections related to him.

I traveled through several Air Force bases on my way to Vietnam, including Travis Air Force Base in California. From there, I flew to Hawaii and then Okinawa before heading to Vietnam. The Air Force was part of my transit, but I did not serve in it. Their bases were key points in my deployment route.

Travis Air Force Base in California was one of the stops on my way to Vietnam. I flew from there to Hawaii, then Okinawa, before arriving in Vietnam. It was part of the long journey from the United States to the combat zone. I remember the heat and the anticipation as we moved closer to the war zone.

Hawaii was a stopover point during my deployment to Vietnam. After leaving Travis Air Force Base, we flew to Hawaii before continuing on to Okinawa and then Vietnam. It was part of the journey but I didn't stay long. It was a brief pause before heading into combat.

Okinawa was another stop on my way to Vietnam where we received our jungle fatigues and dress clothes were packed away. We only kept what we needed for the jungle environment, like socks and fatigues. It was a preparation point before heading into the combat zone. From Okinawa, we flew directly to Vietnam.

I was initially told I might be sent to Guantanamo Bay Naval Base in Cuba, but the guys talked me out of it. They said it would be mostly guard duty and spit-shine work, which didn't appeal to me. Instead, I chose to go to Vietnam. So, I never served at Guantanamo Bay, but it was an option early on.

Laos was not a place I served or mentioned in my military experience. My service was focused on Vietnam, specifically locations like Phu Bai, Khe Sanh, and the Defense of Hue. I have no direct connection to Laos during my time in the military.
